answers.value: Failure to properly secure cleaning chemicals and equipment in a patient care area (4th Floor East, Med‑Surg Unit) at end of shift on 10/10/2025. A cart containing disinfectants and sharps container bags was left unattended in a hallway accessible to visitors and pediatric patients for approximately 35 minutes. Incident investigated by Environmental Services management and Risk Management; no patient harm identified but classified as a serious safety violation. Employee received refresher training on safety and storage protocols on 10/15/2025.

answers.field_label: Describe In Detail The Basis Or Bases For The Recommended Disciplinary Action
answers.value:
This recommendation for a fifteen (15) calendar day suspension without pay for Mr. Marcus D. Holloway, Environmental Services Technician II, is based on a pattern of ongoing performance and conduct issues that have continued despite prior progressive discipline, as well as a recent serious incident involving disregard for established safety and infection‑control procedures in a patient care environment.

1. EMPLOYEE ROLE AND EXPECTATIONS

Mr. Holloway has been employed as an Environmental Services Technician II at John H. Stroger, Jr. Hospital since November 2019. In this role, he is responsible for cleaning and sanitizing patient rooms, restrooms, hallways, and clinical support areas; properly handling and disposing of biohazardous and regular waste; restocking supplies; and maintaining compliance with infection‑control and safety standards as outlined in department policies and Joint Commission requirements.

The position description, which Mr. Holloway signed upon hire and again during his 2024 performance review, emphasizes that:
- Work must be completed in accordance with established schedules and assignments.
- All chemicals and equipment must be handled and stored in compliance with departmental policies and OSHA guidelines.
- Patient and staff safety, as well as confidentiality and respect for patients, are critical components of the job.

2. SUMMARY OF PRIOR DISCIPLINE AND COACHING

Mr. Holloway has been the subject of multiple prior disciplinary actions within the past twelve (12) months:

• March 18, 2025 – Verbal Counseling (documented)
  - Basis: Repeated tardiness to the 7:00 a.m.–3:30 p.m. shift on three occasions (3/3, 3/7, and 3/14/2025), each between 10 and 18 minutes late, without prior notification.
  - Action: Supervisor, Ms. Tanya Richardson, met with Mr. Holloway and verbally counseled him on attendance expectations, the importance of reporting anticipated lateness before the start of the shift, and the impact of late arrivals on coverage for patient room turnovers. He was advised that continued attendance issues could result in formal discipline.

• July 2, 2025 – Written Warning (Attendance / Call‑Off Procedures)
  - Basis: One unexcused absence (6/24/2025) and a late call‑off made less than one hour before shift start (6/28/2025), with the employee contacting a co‑worker instead of the designated attendance line.
  - Action: Mr. Holloway received a written warning. The attendance and call‑off policy was reviewed line by line with him. He was provided a copy of the policy and signed the written warning acknowledging understanding and receipt. He was explicitly told that further occurrences could lead to suspension.

• October 19, 2025 – Three‑Day Suspension Without Pay (Safety Violation)
  - Basis: On October 10, 2025, Mr. Holloway left a cleaning cart unattended and unsecured in a Med‑Surg corridor (4th Floor East) for approximately 35 minutes at the end of his shift. The cart contained disinfectant chemicals, mop buckets, and bags for sharps container disposal. This area is routinely accessed by visitors, including pediatric patients. Risk Management classified the incident as a serious safety violation, though no injury was reported.
  - Action: Mr. Holloway served a three‑day suspension (10/21–10/23/2025). Upon his return, he completed a refresher training module on safe storage of chemicals and equipment, and he signed an acknowledgment that any future safety or infection‑control violations could result in more severe discipline, including extended suspension or termination.

Despite these progressive measures, Mr. Holloway has continued to engage in conduct that falls below departmental standards, culminating in the incident described below.

3. CURRENT INCIDENT LEADING TO THIS RECOMMENDATION

Date of Incident: January 9, 2026  
Location: 6th Floor South – Medical Oncology Unit, Rooms 612–620  
Shift: 7:00 a.m. – 3:30 p.m.  
Employee Assignment: Daily cleaning and discharge turnover for patient rooms 614, 616, 618, and 620, plus hallway and utility closet areas.

At approximately 1:45 p.m. on January 9, 2026, charge nurse Maria Lopez, RN, reported to Environmental Services supervision that Room 618, which had been marked as “cleaned” and ready for a new admission, was not in acceptable condition. Specifically, she observed:
- Visible dried bodily fluid (suspected blood) on the lower side rail of the bed and on a portion of the floor near the foot of the bed.
- A used pair of disposable gloves on the bedside table.
- A partially filled red biohazard bag tied but left on the floor near the bathroom door instead of being removed from the room and placed in the designated soiled utility area.

The room had been vacated by the prior patient at approximately 12:25 p.m. and was assigned to Mr. Holloway for discharge cleaning. The unit clerk’s log and bed‑tracking system (TeleTracking) indicate that Mr. Holloway scanned into Room 618 at 12:38 p.m. and scanned out at 12:59 p.m. At 1:38 p.m., he updated the room status in the system as “clean/ready.”

At 2:05 p.m., Environmental Services Supervisor, Ms. Richardson, and Infection Prevention staff member, James Patel, RN, CIC, conducted an immediate inspection of Room 618. Their observations were consistent with Nurse Lopez’s report:
- Dried red‑brown staining on the bed rail and floor (photographed and documented by Infection Prevention).
- Used gloves on the overbed table.
- Red biohazard bag containing used linens and disposable items on the floor.
- High‑touch surfaces (bed rail controls, call light button, and door handle) tested with ATP swabs showed readings above the acceptable threshold for a terminally cleaned room.

Mr. Holloway was called to the unit and asked to walk through his cleaning process. He initially stated that he “went through the checklist” and that “the room looked fine” when he left. When asked specifically whether he checked and wiped down the bed rails and removed all trash and biohazard waste, he admitted that he “might have rushed a bit” because he knew another discharge was expected in Room 620 and wanted to “get ahead” on his assignments.

4. POLICY VIOLATIONS

The conduct described above violates multiple policies and procedures that Mr. Holloway has previously been trained on and acknowledged:

a. Environmental Services Policy EVS‑101: Discharge and Terminal Cleaning of Patient Rooms
- Requires that all visibly soiled surfaces, including bed rails, bedside tables, call buttons, and floors, be thoroughly cleaned and disinfected using approved hospital disinfectants.
- Requires that all waste, including biohazard bags, be removed from the room and transported to designated disposal areas.
- Requires completion of a standardized checklist before marking a room as “clean/ready” in the bed‑tracking system.

b. Infection Control Policy IC‑203: Standard Precautions and Environmental Cleaning
- Mandates removal and proper disposal of used personal protective equipment (PPE), including gloves, after each patient encounter and prior to leaving the room.
- Emphasizes that failure to remove visible bodily fluids and contaminated materials from patient care areas significantly increases the risk of healthcare‑associated infections (HAIs).

c. Work Performance and Conduct Standards
- Employees are required to perform duties with reasonable care, thoroughness, and attention to patient safety.
- Employees must not misrepresent the status of work performed (e.g., marking a room as “clean/ready” when required cleaning tasks have not been properly completed).

By marking Room 618 as “clean/ready” while leaving visible bodily fluid on surfaces, failing to remove used PPE and a biohazard bag, and not adequately disinfecting high‑touch areas, Mr. Holloway did not meet the minimum safety and performance standards for his position.

5. RISK AND IMPACT

Although there is no documented case of patient harm directly linked to this specific incident, the potential risk is significant. The Medical Oncology Unit cares for immunocompromised patients who are particularly vulnerable to infection. Admitting a new patient into a room where bodily fluids remain on surfaces and high‑touch areas have not been properly disinfected creates an unacceptable risk for:
- Bloodborne pathogen exposure.
- Transmission of multi‑drug resistant organisms (MDROs).
- General healthcare‑associated infections that can extend hospital stays and increase costs.

Additionally, the incident undermines the trust between nursing staff and Environmental Services. Nursing staff rely on accurate room status updates to place patients. When a room is inaccurately marked as clean, it delays admissions, disrupts patient flow, and causes additional workload for both nursing and Environmental Services staff who must re‑clean the room under time pressure.

6. EMPLOYEE RESPONSE AND MITIGATING/AGGRAVATING FACTORS

During the investigatory meeting held on January 13, 2026, with Ms. Richardson (supervisor) and HR Representative Daniel Ortiz present, Mr. Holloway acknowledged that he did not fully inspect the bed rails and floor area and that he should have removed the red biohazard bag from the room. He stated that he felt “behind” on his assignments and was trying to “move quickly so the nurses could get patients in.”

Mitigating factors:
- Mr. Holloway expressed remorse and stated that he did not intend to put any patient at risk.
- He cooperated with the investigation and did not dispute the factual findings.
- His overall attendance has slightly improved since the written warning in July 2025, with no additional unexcused absences in the last three months.

Aggravating factors:
- Mr. Holloway has a recent history of a serious safety violation (October 2025 chemical/equipment cart incident) and was explicitly warned that further safety‑related issues could lead to more severe discipline.
- The current incident occurred less than three months after completion of a safety refresher training.
- The nature of the work (cleaning rooms for immunocompromised patients) requires heightened diligence and adherence to protocol. Rushing through required steps is not acceptable.

7. RATIONALE FOR RECOMMENDED DISCIPLINE LEVEL

Progressive discipline has already been applied in Mr. Holloway’s case, including documented verbal counseling, a written warning, and a three‑day suspension. Despite these interventions and additional training, he has again engaged in conduct that compromises patient safety and violates clearly established procedures.

Given the seriousness of the infection‑control lapse and the recurrence of safety‑related issues within a relatively short timeframe, management considered recommending termination. However, taking into account his cooperation, expressed remorse, and the absence of a documented patient injury from this specific incident, the department is instead recommending a significant suspension of fifteen (15) calendar days without pay as the next step in progressive discipline.

This level of discipline is intended to:
- Underscore the seriousness of infection‑control and safety compliance.
- Provide a final opportunity for Mr. Holloway to correct his behavior and demonstrate consistent adherence to policies.
- Protect patients and staff by signaling that similar lapses will not be tolerated.

8. EXPECTATIONS UPON RETURN TO WORK

If this recommendation is approved and the suspension is imposed, Mr. Holloway will be required to:
- Complete a remedial training session on EVS discharge/terminal cleaning procedures and infection‑control policies IC‑203 and related modules before returning to independent assignments.
- Work under closer supervision for a period of at least sixty (60) days following his return, including random spot checks of rooms he has cleaned.
- Maintain full compliance with attendance, safety, and performance standards, with the understanding that any further serious safety, infection‑control, or integrity‑related violations may result in termination.

In summary, the recommended fifteen (15) day suspension without pay is based on Mr. Holloway’s repeated failure to follow critical safety and infection‑control procedures, his history of prior related discipline, and the significant risk his actions pose to a vulnerable patient population, balanced against his cooperation and stated willingness to improve.
